frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Happy Mother's Day to the woman who is my entire world 🙏❤️ Aaj subah uthke socha ki kya likhunga, aur itna kuch hai ki words kam pad rahe hain. Maa, I still remember those hands — rough from years of washing, chopping, kneading — hands that never rested. Every single morning, without fail, the smell of her tadka would wake me up before any alarm ever could. She would stand at that stove from 6am, making sure everyone's tiffin was packed, everyone's chai was hot, never sitting down to eat until the last person in the house had finished. When I was in college and used to come home at midnight after studying, she would be sitting in the dark in the hall, waiting, not saying a word, just waiting to see my face. Not once — not ONCE — did she say 'I'm tired' or 'Why so late' or 'Think about me.' Her kitchen was her kingdom and she ruled it with so much love that even today, at 39, no restaurant, no 5-star hotel, nothing comes close to her dal and rice. She gave up so much — her own needs, her own sleep, her own everything — and she did it all with a smile. This is what real strength looks like. This is what unconditional love looks like. Maa, you are my definition of a perfect woman. Love you forever. 🙏 #MothersDay #MaaKiMmamta #Grateful #MyHero
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The post encodes benevolent sexism through the idealization of a woman whose entire identity is constituted by domestic labor, self-erasure, and silent suffering — framed explicitly as 'perfect womanhood.' The speaker is sincere and emotionally genuine, which makes the misogyny covert: the reduction of a woman's worth to her cooking, her waiting, and her never complaining is presented as tribute rather than constraint.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 39. Married. A man in Mumbai. You are posting on Facebook under your real name on Mother's Day. You are writing a long post about your mother. The post is emotional and specific. But every specific thing you mention is domestic Ñ her hands, her kitchen, her smell of her cooking, the way she stayed up waiting for you, the way she never complained, the way she put everyone first. You are writing this as a tribute. You do not notice that the woman you are describing has no identity outside her kitchen and her self-erasure and that you are presenting this as the ideal of womanhood to your 400 Facebook friends.
